ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pigeon meat food product entry; no specific Cantonese roast pigeon entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN covers pigeon meat generically; no specific code for the Cantonese roast/deep-fried preparation exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for roast pigeon or squab as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Cantonese Roast Pigeon is approximately 180 g, equivalent to roughly half a whole young pigeon, as commonly served in Cantonese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Squab (pigeon), meat and skin, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw squab (pigeon) meat and skin used as the primary nutritional anchor. Values have been adapted to reflect the cooked, glazed, and deep-fried Cantonese preparation, including increased sodium from seasoning rub and minor carbohydrate contribution from the honey-vinegar glaze. Saturated fat estimated from USDA squab lipid profile. Detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat) and water content set to -1 as specific cooked-preparation data are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based dishes — analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate poultry preparation
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cantonese Roast Pigeon in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on the analogous category of high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked poultry dishes. The dish is predominantly protein and fat; the only carbohydrate source is a small honey-vinegar glaze contributing approximately 1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic load per 180 g serving = (15 × 1.5 × 1.8) / 100 = 0.41, rounded to 0.4. This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance that protein- and fat-dominant foods have negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg per 100 g) from seasoning rub, master stock, and dipping salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Cantonese Roast Pigeon due to its high sodium content; if consumed, remove the skin, avoid the spiced-salt dip, and restrict portion size to reduce sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.5 g per 100 g) with negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cantonese Roast Pigeon is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic load; however, total calorie and saturated fat intake should be monitored, and the skin should be removed to reduce fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat content and high sodium from deep-frying and seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Cantonese Roast Pigeon sparingly; removing the skin significantly reduces saturated fat and sodium intake, and the dipping salt should be avoided to minimise cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roast Pigeon (红烧乳鸽, Hóngshāo rǔgē), also known as Cantonese Roast Squab, is a celebrated Cantonese delicacy originating from Guangdong Province, China. The dish features a young pigeon (squab) that is first blanched in a fragrant master stock, then glazed with a mixture of honey and vinegar to impart colour and a subtle sweetness, before being deep-fried or roasted at high heat to achieve its signature paper-thin, lacquered, and intensely crispy skin. The meat is dark, succulent, and rich, with a slightly gamey depth of flavour enhanced by a dry rub of salt and Chinese five-spice powder. It is typically served whole or halved, accompanied by prawn crackers and a small dish of spiced salt and lemon juice for dipping. Nutritionally, Roast Pigeon is a high-protein, moderate-fat food providing approximately 215 kcal, 18.5 g protein, and 14.8 g fat per 100 g, with very low carbohydrate content (1.5 g per 100 g). It is a notable source of iron (4.2 mg), niacin (6.2 mg), vitamin B6 (0.45 mg), and zinc (2.5 mg). The sodium content is relatively high at 450 mg per 100 g due to the seasoning rub and master stock. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic impact, but should be consumed with caution by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease owing to its sodium and fat content. It is a prestige dish served at Cantonese banquets and special occasions.

meal_context: Gourmet Cantonese restaurant lunch or dinner

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw squab (pigeon) meat and skin, adjusted for the Cantonese cooked preparation. No exact match for the fully prepared dish exists in any approved primary source. Saturated fat is estimated from the USDA squab lipid profile. Detailed fatty acid fractions and water content are unavailable for this specific preparation. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (squab, meat and skin, raw) to reflect the cooked, glazed, and deep-fried Cantonese preparation. Sodium is elevated relative to raw squab due to the seasoning rub, master stock, and dipping condiments. Carbohydrate reflects the minor contribution of the honey-vinegar glaze. 2. Saturated fat (4.2 g per 100 g) is estimated from the USDA squab lipid profile; this value should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis of the prepared dish. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for the Cantonese roast/deep-fried pigeon preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2024. 4. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero (pigeon meat contains no dietary fibre). vitamin_c_mg is naturally zero (cooked poultry meat contains negligible vitamin C per USDA FoodData Central). 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database guidance for high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate poultry dishes; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 6. The input dietary_tags incorrectly included 'vegetarian'; this has been corrected — Roast Pigeon is not vegetarian. 7. Duplicate portion entry in input has been deduplicated to a single 180 g serving. 8. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry exists for Cantonese Roast Pigeon.
